location.name: done
location.label: x'==0
We stop the clock x here, so that we could inspect it later.
x<=delay && delay'==0
Here the variable delay is of type clock, we need to stop it to create a static bound on x.

transition.label: delay=f(), x=0
Generates a delay bound based on f() distribution.

template.name: P2
template.declaration: clock x; double delay; // instead of clock

location.label: x<=delay

Here the delay variable is of double type and do not progress with time, thus no need to stop it.
The model is simpler but slower: Uppaal treats it as ODE model (due to double variable), and uses small step integration, which slows down the simulation significantly.

transition.label: delay=f(), x=0
Generates a delay bound based on f() distribution.

query.comment: Let's wait for the mean delay and estimate what is the probability of reaching done. Expect ~0.5 and the probability density distribution to be half of the bell curve. Set the statistical parameter ε to 0.005 or smaller for more data and better resolution.
The same as above, except using double instead of stopwatch. Uncomment "system P1, P2" in the system declaration to include process P2.

Generate runs of up to 100 time units (far beond the f() distribution) and estimate the final/largest value of x. Expect ~10.0, and full bell curve around 10 in the probability density distribution plot.
we can also inspect the distribution of the delay variable without waiting for the actual delay.
